questions.question: Why are retries a first-class design input for location pipelines?
options: Because weak networks can amplify duplicate sends, so dedupe capacity becomes part of normal operation.
Because retries only matter for analytics and never for live serving.
Because location events are always globally ordered by default.
Because mobile clients cannot attach timestamps or identifiers.
questions.explanation: Mobile and IoT senders often retry aggressively under poor connectivity.

questions.question: What is the main value of a monotonic client sequence number on updates?
options: It gives a stronger signal for duplicate and out-of-order detection than timestamps alone.
It compresses GPS payloads for edge caches.
It replaces the need for any dedupe state.
It guarantees exact clock synchronization with the server.
questions.explanation: A sequence number makes ordering and stale-drop decisions far more credible.

questions.question: What is the strongest reason to publish a live-state projection separate from the raw event stream?
options: Serving paths need one trusted latest state, while replay and analytics want fuller event history.
Raw streams cannot be stored durably once they are consumed.
A live-state projection removes the need for freshness monitoring.
Analytics systems cannot read location data directly.
questions.explanation: Different consumers want different shapes of truth from the same pipeline.

questions.question: Which metric best exposes an unhealthy ordering story?
options: Out-of-order discard rate and accepted-update age by partition or region.
The number of engineers subscribed to the pipeline team chat.
The count of configured API routes in the gateway repository.
The ratio of encrypted requests to total requests on the company network.
questions.explanation: Ordering and freshness pathologies appear directly in discard and age metrics.

questions.question: Why can GPS smoothing be risky if overused?
options: It can hide true movement or delay sharp changes even while making paths look cleaner.
It forces every update into a single database transaction.
It prevents duplicate detection from working.
It guarantees worse network cost than raw updates.
questions.explanation: Smoothing trades noisy accuracy for stability, so it can also mask reality.

questions.question: What is the most credible behavior when one region's pipeline lags far behind the freshness target?
options: Fail data stale and surface freshness state rather than silently serving old positions as current.
Keep serving the oldest available state without any expiry logic.
Pause all global regions so consistency stays perfectly matched.
Drop all analytics writes permanently to protect appearance.
questions.explanation: Silent stale data is usually worse than an explicit degraded state.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with keeping a longer late-arrival window?
options: It absorbs more network disorder but complicates serving freshness and state correction.
It removes the need for dedupe windows entirely.
It guarantees lower memory use in stream consumers.
It eliminates the need for sequence checks.
questions.explanation: Accepting more disorder helps ingestion but makes live truth harder to maintain.

questions.question: Which redesign is most likely if downstream fraud detection must inspect all raw points but nearby search needs only the latest accepted state?
options: Keep dual products: a durable raw stream for analysis and a bounded live-state projection for reads.
Stop smoothing entirely so both systems can share one exact read path.
Serve nearby search directly from the analytics warehouse.
Delete all rejected updates to reduce legal risk.
questions.explanation: One pipeline can feed multiple products, but not all of them should share the same read model.
